evidence: goseg/protocol/actions/actions.go confines protocol action metadata, namespace dispatch, and action-compatibility helpers to a single focused file with explicit domain names (`NamespaceUpload`, `NamespaceC2C`, `UploadPayload`) and no broad cross-package coupling beyond `groundseg/protocol/contracts`.

`goseg/startram/errors.go` keeps startram error contract ownership with a single descriptor and wrapper type (`APIConnectionErrorContract`, `APIConnectionError`) and does not introduce global mutable state or module-level side effects.
Both files avoid import-time initialization logic beyond static constant/var declarations and rely on value-based copy returns (`copy`, `make`) to keep exported snapshots encapsulated.
high_level_elegance.issues_preventing_higher_score: No blocking high-level architectural defects were observed in the assigned scope; further gains would likely be minor naming or API-shape tightening rather than structural decompositional issues.

evidence: Multiple modules expose callback/adapter seams built from one-method interfaces and function adapters even when there is a single concrete implementation path (for example `startupRuntime` adapters in `goseg/startup_orchestrator.go`, `broadcastRuntimeContract` adapters in `goseg/broadcast/broadcast.go`, and `importerRuntime` adapters in `goseg/importer/importer.go`).
Runtime graphs are composed through repetitive `withDefaults`/constructor chains instead of cohesive domain-specific dependency containers, increasing call depth and hiding actual coupling.
The same pattern appears in `goseg/docker/orchestration/wireguard.go` (`wireguardRuntime` + `wireguardRuntimeFromDocker`) where seam plumbing dominates logic and lowers signal-to-policy ratio.
`startBackgroundServicesWithRuntime` runs `runtime.syncRetrieveFn()` inline before service supervision and logs only a warning if it fails.

The same startup path then starts supervised services (`startStartramRenewalReminder`, `startDocker*`, loop services) as a background group in `startBackgroundServicesWithRuntime`, so retrieval failures can be masked while downstream routines continue.
`Bootstrap` delegates to `startBackgroundServices` from `goseg/main.go`, and the runtime callback contract allows this warning-only behavior even in `startramWgRegistered` mode.

`StartVersionSubsystemWithContext`/`CheckVersionLoopWithContext` pass context to loops (`goseg/routines/version.go`), but update execution uses `http.Get(url)` without request context in `callUpdater`.
`verifySlsaProvenance` also uses `http.Get(provenanceURL)` directly, so verifier/download paths are not cancelable and can ignore shutdown-driven cancellation.
The module already defines context-aware entrypoints, but network IO bypasses them in the critical update path.

The same module set mixes lifecycle orchestration, runtime configuration, transport dispatch, and protocol payload/state model responsibilities in a single file, increasing coupling across unrelated behavior.
Docker-related orchestration/runtime packages repeat multiple independent runtime-composition abstractions instead of reusing a shared seam.
Several files define duplicated configuration/adapter wrappers for similar container/write-to-volume workflows with only package-local naming differences.

findings.summary: Seam patterns add indirection without policy, especially in startup and transport/runtime wiring.
evidence: `startupRuntime` defines multiple one-method interfaces plus function-adapter types, merged field-by-field.
`broadcastRuntimeContract`/`broadcastRuntimeFns` is a context-free callback wrapper layer with repeated re-wiring in option helpers.
`importerRuntime` and `wireguardRuntime` repeat the same contract-to-fn adapter pattern with minimal logic added.
findings.suggestion: Consolidate seam design into a small set of domain-specific dependency structs per startup/runtime layer (e.g., bootstrap subsystem, background orchestration, and wireguard import/load), and keep interface abstractions only where multiple implementations are truly expected; remove adapter duplication that simply forwards function calls.

findings.summary: Synchronous Startram sync is run outside the supervised startup service graph and treated as non-fatal.
evidence: `startBackgroundServicesWithRuntime` executes `runtime.syncRetrieveFn()` before adding background services.
When `syncRetrieveFn` returns error, code only logs and continues startup with `logger.Warn`.
The same startup flow also starts `startStartramRenewalReminder` and transition handlers in the background service set.
findings.suggestion: Run sync retrieval as a supervised/first-class startup phase (`superviseBackgroundService`-style) and return startup failure on first hard-sync error in `startramWgRegistered` mode, or add an explicit readiness gate with bounded retry + hard-fail fallback so downstream state is not silently started with potentially stale discovery data.

findings.summary: Version update downloads ignore shutdown context, weakening lifecycle and cancellation behavior.
evidence: `callUpdater` performs `http.Get(url)` for binary download with no context or timeout.
`verifySlsaProvenance` also uses `http.Get(provenanceURL)` and fixed temporary files.
The call stack originates from context-aware startup paths (`StartVersionSubsystemWithContext`), but network calls do not propagate cancellation.
findings.suggestion: Inject a version-update runtime HTTP client with `http.Client{Timeout: ...}` and use `http.NewRequestWithContext` in both `callUpdater` and `verifySlsaProvenance` so shutdown/cancel signals stop in-flight fetches and avoid blocking shutdown paths.

findings.summary: Config init mixes persistence concerns with system-level startup wiring, blurring package ownership
evidence: `bootstrap.go` calls `configureFixerScript()` inside `initializeConfig()`, and that function delegates to `system.FixerScript`.
The same package is also responsible for mutating readiness/state (for example `Ready`, `initOnce`) and runtime wiring, rather than exposing a pure configuration-loading API.
findings.suggestion: Refactor startup so `initializeConfig()` only handles config loading/validation and returns a config/result object; move `system.FixerScript` and any other side-effectful setup to an orchestration startup layer that calls config initialization as one phase and system bootstrap as the next.

findings.summary: Config process context has two mutable ownership paths, enabling stale or inconsistent runtime state
evidence: `config.go` defines global mutable fields (`BasePath`, `Architecture`, `DockerDir`, `DebugMode`) and also a separate `runtimeContext` store.
`applyRuntimeContext` writes into both the context mutex-protected field and package globals, and globals are read directly across package entry points.
`initializePaths` recalculates `confPath`/`keyPath` from `RuntimeContextSnapshot()`, adding another state mutation path in addition to global updates.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a single source of truth for execution context by removing duplicated globals from public package state, passing `RuntimeContext` through explicit call boundaries, and making `Conf()`/runtime accessors read from that single context object.

findings.summary: Exported API contract descriptor is mutable, weakening contract ownership guarantees.
evidence: `goseg/startram/errors.go` defines `APIConnectionErrorContract` as an exported `var`, not a read-only contract object.
The README establishes this file as a governance point of truth for protocol/error contract surfaces; mutability in a governance file undermines that boundary.
findings.suggestion: Make the contract descriptor immutable to callers (e.g., keep contract data in an unexported `var` and expose `func APIConnectionErrorContract() APIConnectionErrorContractDescriptor` or return literal values from dedicated accessors), so external packages cannot alter compatibility policy by assignment.

findings.summary: Contract compatibility vocabulary is inconsistent across governance surfaces.
evidence: `goseg/protocol/actions/actions.go` exports compatibility constants as `CompatibilityStable`/`CompatibilityBackwardSafe`, while `goseg/startram/errors.go` uses `ErrorContractStable`/`ErrorContractBackwardSafe` for the same concept.
README frames these files as canonical contract definitions, so divergent naming increases cognitive load for maintainers working across protocol and error contract domains.
findings.suggestion: Standardize contract vocabulary and naming (for example, use a single `Compatibility*` terminology across both files, or centralize compatibility aliases in a shared contract package and re-export consistently) to keep governance semantics predictable.

findings.summary: Auth package bundles lifecycle orchestration with token crypto and websocket auth flows in one file, obscuring ownership.
evidence: `goseg/auth/auth.go` owns startup lifecycle (`Initialize`, `Start`, `Stop`, `runAuthCleanupLoop`) and also token serialization/cipher handling (`KeyfileEncrypt`, `KeyfileDecrypt`, `AuthorizeUploadToken`, `CreateToken`).

`goseg/structs/ws.go` also exposes websocket session primitives (`ClientManager`, `MuConn`) used by auth while carrying protocol payload contracts, widening the same domain into low-level transport + policy concerns.
findings.suggestion: Split auth into separate modules (for example `auth/lifecycle`, `auth/token_validation`, and `auth/session`), and keep websocket session primitives in a dedicated structs or session package with explicit interfaces for the auth boundary.

findings.summary: Broadcast file conflates stateful domain model management with transport emission concerns.
evidence: `goseg/broadcast/broadcast.go` defines global mutable state (`broadcastState`, schedule maps, mutexes), transition application helpers, and websocket/leak fan-out entrypoints in the same package surface.
It also handles state cloning and serialization in the same file as transport dispatch (`BroadcastToClients`, `UnauthBroadcast`), creating an intertwined handoff path.
findings.suggestion: Split `broadcast.go` into `state` (durable state machine, cloning, mutation invariants), `transitions` (intent encoding), and `delivery` (broadcast transport + retry/drop policy) to keep handoff seams explicit.

findings.summary: Runtime seam patterns are reimplemented across docker packages instead of converging on one shared abstraction.
evidence: `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_seams.go` and `goseg/docker/lifecycle/lifecycle.go` both define separate `Runtime` types with option adapters and default adapter injection.

`goseg/docker/orchestration/container/helpers.go` and `goseg/docker/orchestration/volume_writer.go` duplicate `writeConfigArtifact`/volume bootstrap wrapper patterns rather than reusing a single internal seam.
`goseg/docker/orchestration/container/netdata.go` composes its own artifact-writing options against these near-equivalent patterns, increasing drift risk.
findings.suggestion: Extract a shared internal runtime/composition primitive for adapter wiring and artifact-plan execution, then migrate both lifecycle and orchestration call paths to it before adding more container-specific runtimes.
